steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis/biliary obstruction; indeterminate appendiceal finding (distended tip 12mm without fat stranding at outside CT, limited by body habitus)
ensemble.information_gap: The outside CT was explicitly quoted as 'very limited' given body habitus, leaving both the appendiceal finding (distended tip up to 12mm, no fat stranding) and the gallbladder pathology (distended GB with stones and subtle fat stranding) incompletely characterized; the RUQ ultrasound at the intermediate facility showed GB wall thickening and stones but could not evaluate the appendix or exclude choledocholithiasis definitively, so the anatomic source of RUQ/RLQ pain remains unresolved.
ensemble.expected_finding: Either periappendiceal fat stranding/fluid confirming acute appendicitis, or pericholecystic fluid/GB wall edema confirming acute cholecystitis, or a normal appendix and gallbladder that would redirect the workup; also hoping to characterize the CBD more completely than ultrasound allowed.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The outside CT was explicitly limited by body habitus and left two competing diagnoses (appendicitis vs. cholecystitis) unresolved, the RUQ ultrasound could not evaluate the appendix, and the patient has ongoing pain with leukocytosis and neutrophilia, making a dedicated high-quality CT with contrast at this institution the logical next step to definitively characterize both the appendix and the biliary system.
grounding: HPI: 'CT scan of abdomen and pelvis w/IV contrast which showed a distended tip of appendix upto 12mm without fat stranding, distended gallbladder containing multiple stones with subtle fat stranding. These findings were however quoted as very limited given his body habitus.'
HPI: 'RUQ US showed GB wall thickening to 4.2mm, multiple gallstones, CBD 4mm.'
HPI: 'RUQ and RLQ abdominal pain starting around ___ evening'
Physical exam: 'Obese, Soft, nondistended, active bowel sounds. Appropriately tender to palpation, no rebound or guarding.'
HPI: 'morbidly obese, quadriplegic/expressive aphasia' — limits reliability of physical exam
ensemble.reasoning: I have two plausible surgical diagnoses — early/equivocal appendicitis (distended appendiceal tip 12mm, no fat stranding) and acute cholecystitis (GB wall thickening, stones, subtle fat stranding) — but the outside imaging was explicitly degraded by morbid obesity and the ultrasound could not evaluate the appendix at all. With ongoing pain, leukocytosis with left shift, and a patient whose quadriplegia and expressive aphasia limit the reliability of the abdominal exam, I need a high-quality CT with IV contrast at this institution to definitively characterize both the appendix and the gallbladder/biliary tree before committing to a management plan, especially given his supratherapeutic INR which makes any operative intervention high-risk.

verification.actual_finding: The CT showed gallstones without pericholecystic stranding or GB wall edema, a mildly dilated appendix tip at 9 mm without periappendiceal fat stranding or free fluid, no biliary ductal dilatation, and no intraperitoneal free fluid.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The scan partially resolved the information gap by better characterizing both organs and excluding choledocholithiasis, though it yielded an indeterminate rather than definitively positive or negative result for either appendicitis or cholecystitis, which the physician had identified as one of the three possible outcomes hoped for.
